subject: ap-psychology
theme: Development and learning examine how cognitive, social, and moral capacities change across the lifespan (Piaget, Vygotsky, Erikson, Bronfenbrenner) and how behavior is acquired through classical conditioning, operant conditioning, and observational learning.

core_concepts.title: Piaget's Cognitive Development Stages
core_concepts.detail: Piaget's four stages: Sensorimotor (0-2 yrs — object permanence, A-not-B error); Preoperational (2-7 yrs — symbolic thought, egocentrism, failure of conservation, animism); Concrete Operational (7-11 yrs — conservation, reversibility, classification); Formal Operational (11+ yrs — abstract and hypothetical reasoning). Development proceeds through assimilation (fitting into existing schemas) and accommodation (modifying schemas for new information).

core_concepts.title: Vygotsky's Sociocultural Theory
core_concepts.detail: Vygotsky's sociocultural theory emphasizes the zone of proximal development (ZPD) — the gap between what a learner can do alone and with expert guidance. Scaffolding (temporary support) bridges the ZPD. Language and thought are intertwined; private speech (talking to oneself) guides learning.

core_concepts.title: Attachment Styles
core_concepts.detail: Attachment (Ainsworth's Strange Situation): secure (explores freely, consoled at reunion); anxious-ambivalent (intense distress, inconsolable at reunion); avoidant (no distress, ignores caregiver); disorganized (contradictory behaviors). Harlow's rhesus monkey studies showed contact comfort — not feeding — is the basis of attachment.

core_concepts.title: Parenting Styles & Ecological Systems
core_concepts.detail: Parenting styles (Baumrind): authoritative (high warmth + high control → best outcomes); authoritarian (low warmth + high control); permissive (high warmth + low control); uninvolved (low warmth + low control → worst outcomes). Bronfenbrenner's ecological systems theory describes microsystem (direct interactions), mesosystem (connections between microsystems), exosystem (indirect influences), macrosystem (cultural values), and chronosystem (time and historical change).

core_concepts.title: Erikson's Psychosocial Stages
core_concepts.detail: Erikson's psychosocial stages: Trust vs. Mistrust (infancy); Autonomy vs. Shame (toddler); Initiative vs. Guilt (preschool); Industry vs. Inferiority (school-age); Identity vs. Role Confusion (adolescence); Intimacy vs. Isolation (young adult). Marcia's identity statuses: achievement (explored + committed), moratorium (exploring, not committed), foreclosure (committed without exploration), diffusion (neither).

core_concepts.title: Classical Conditioning
core_concepts.detail: Classical conditioning (Pavlov): Unconditioned Stimulus → Unconditioned Response; Conditioned Stimulus (paired with UCS) → Conditioned Response. Key processes: acquisition, extinction, spontaneous recovery, stimulus generalization, stimulus discrimination. Garcia effect: taste-illness associations are learned despite long delays — biological preparedness constrains conditioning.

core_concepts.title: Operant & Observational Learning
core_concepts.detail: Operant conditioning (Skinner): behavior is shaped by consequences. Reinforcement (positive: add pleasant; negative: remove aversive) increases behavior. Punishment decreases behavior. Schedules: fixed-ratio (high rate, post-reinforcement pause); variable-ratio (highest, most resistant — slot machines); fixed-interval (scallop pattern); variable-interval (steady, moderate rate). Shaping reinforces successive approximations. Observational learning (Bandura): vicarious reinforcement, attention, retention, reproduction, and motivation are required for learning from models.

exam_tip: FRQ questions commonly ask you to apply a specific learning principle (shaping, negative reinforcement, VR schedule) to a real-world scenario — ensure you correctly distinguish negative reinforcement (increases behavior) from punishment (decreases behavior).
